title: Smart Home Monitoring Dashboard
text:
This project focuses on monitoring and visualizing live data from my apartment. The goal was to create a centralized hub..



Real-time Monitoring: Live tracking of temperature, humidity, and door/window status.
Historical Analysis: Long-term data storage to identify seasonal trends.
Public Transport and Bike Sharing Integration





Visualization: Grafana
Database: InfluxDB
Data Source: Home Assistant / Custom sensors




Live Dashboard Demo






The data is collected via various sensors and pushed into a time-series database. Grafana queries this data to display interactive panels. I optimized the layout for a 10-inch wall-mounted tablet to ensure high readability at a glance.



You can find the full source code on GitHub:
View on GitHub

title: About me
text:
I am a Business Informatics student at TU Dresden, specializing in Machine Learning. Currently, I work as a Working Student at Dometa, where I focus on automating building intelligence with supportive API scripts. I transform sensor data into actionable insights using real-time Thingsboard or Grafana dashboards.
With a professional background in Logistics Planning, I combine domain expertise with modern data science to build efficient, automated solutions.
Data Science – Specialized in predictive modeling and ML (Study focus). Logistics Expertise – Deep domain knowledge from my previous career path. Automation – Passionate about Smart Home, APIs, and Python/R scripting. Visualisation – Passionate in creating real-time dashboards with Grafana or Thingsboard.

Check out my Projects to see my work in action or view my CV for the full story.

title: Smart Notifications: Developing a Telegram IoT Bot
text:
Monitoring dashboards is great, but receiving active alerts is better. I developed a Python-based Telegram Bot that acts as a bridge between my home sensors and my smartphone.

Why a Telegram Bot?
Unlike traditional dashboards, a bot can proactively reach out when certain conditions are met. I used the python-telegram-bot library to implement this logic.







Telegram Door Sensor Alarm Bot




Core Features

Real-time Alerts: The bot sends a message if bot is activated (/on) and the window or door is opened.
Status Queries: You can retrieve the status of the window and/or door (/status)



The Technical Stack

Language: Python
API: Telegram Bot API (via python-telegram-bot)
Data Connection: MQTT via Zigbee Dongle


This project demonstrates how to build functional interfaces for data systems, moving from passive monitoring to active automation.


  View Project on GitHub

title: Building a Private Home Dashboard with Grafana
text:
I built a custom home-server environment to monitor my living space in real-time. To achieve this, I connected several temperature and humidity sensors to ESP32 controllers distributed throughout my flat. Additionally, I installed door and window sensors connected via a SONOFF Zigbee dongle.
All sensors transmit their data to a central bridge, which forwards the information into an InfluxDB time-series database.

The Setup
My home automation system collects data from various sensors (temperature, energy consumption, network traffic). The core stack consists of:

Data Sources: Diverse IoT sensors (ESP32-based climate sensors and SONOFF door/window sensors).
Communication: MQTT and an InfluxDB bridge running on a Raspberry Pi.
Database: InfluxDB for efficient time-series storage.
Visualization: Grafana for real-time monitoring and historical analysis.



Key Insights
This dashboard allows me to track environmental changes and room activity precisely. It was an excellent project to practice MQTT protocols, API integration, and data pipeline management before the information reaches the final dashboard.



Grafana Dashboard

title: Extend Home Dashboard with Grafana
text:
To make my Home Dashboard even more practical for daily life, I decided to integrate real-time mobility data. Living in Dresden, I wanted to see exactly when the next tram or bike is available without opening multiple apps.

Integrating Local Transport (DVB)
I extended the dashboard with a live overview of upcoming departures from stations near my flat.

The Tech: I used the DVB.py Python package to interface with the VVO (Verkehrsverbund Oberelbe) data.
The Process: A script fetches the live departure times, filters for relevant lines, and pushes the data into my InfluxDB.




Real Time Public Transport Overview




Bike Sharing Integration (Nextbike)
In addition to public transport, I implemented a tracking feature for Nextbike (the local bike-sharing provider).

API Utilization: By querying the Nextbike API, I can now see the number of available bikes and their exact locations in my immediate vicinity.
Benefit: This allows for a seamless decision-making process in the morning: Tram, bike, or walk?




Real Time Bike Sharing Overview




Technical Takeaway
This extension was a great exercise in:

Working with external APIs and handling JSON data.
Python scripting for automated data retrieval.
Data transformation: Converting live API responses into time-series formats suitable for Grafana.


  View Project on GitHub

title: Legal Notice & Privacy Policy
text:
Privacy Policy (Datenschutzerklärung)

1. General Information
As the operator of this website, I take the protection of your personal data very seriously. I treat your personal data confidentially and in accordance with the statutory data protection regulations (GDPR) and this privacy policy.


2. Data Collection on this Website
This website is hosted by z.B. GitHub Pages. When you visit this site, the hoster automatically collects and stores information in so-called “Server Log Files”, which your browser automatically transmits:

Browser type and version
Operating system used
Referrer URL
Hostname of the accessing computer
Time of the server request
IP address

This data is not combined with other data sources. The basis for data processing is Art. 6 Para. 1 lit. f GDPR, which allows the processing of data for the technically error-free presentation and optimization of the website.


3. Cookies and Tracking
This website does not use tracking cookies (like Google Analytics) or any other personal tracking mechanisms. It is a static website designed for informational purposes only.


4. Your Rights
You have the right at any time to receive information free of charge about the origin, recipient, and purpose of your stored personal data. You also have the right to request the correction, blocking, or deletion of this data.
If you have any questions regarding data protection, you can contact me at the email address provided in the Legal Notice.

Last updated: February 2026

title: Solar PV Monitoring & Data Pipeline
text:
This project focuses on the end-to-end data pipeline required to collect, store, and visualize live metrics from my parents’ photovoltaic (PV) solar system and EV wallbox. The goal was to build a highly responsive, localized monitoring system that aggregates industrial protocols (Modbus) and lightweight IoT messaging (MQTT) into a single centralized dashboard.



Multi-Protocol Data Ingestion: Real-time ingestion of solar inverter metrics via Modbus TCP/RTU and wallbox states via MQTT.
Secure Mesh Routing: All cross-device data traffic between the protocol gateway and the database is fully encrypted and routed over a secure Tailscale network.
Time-Series Storage: High-frequency data logging into InfluxDB, structured for rapid querying and long-term analysis.
UI Refresh Optimization: Fine-tuned panel-specific refresh rates to separate high-frequency live data from slow-moving aggregate metrics.





Data Sources: PV Inverter (Modbus) & EV Wallbox (MQTT)
Edge Collection: Raspberry Pi 2 (Acting as the field protocol gateway)
Central Database: Raspberry Pi 4 running InfluxDB v2 (Time-Series)
Network Overlay: Tailscale (Secure WireGuard-based overlay network)
Visualization: Grafana (Stat panels, time-series graphs, and custom dashboard layouts)




Solar Dashboard Demo

title: Solar PV Monitoring & Data Pipeline
text:
This project focuses on the end-to-end data pipeline required to collect, store, and visualize live metrics from my parents’ photovoltaic (PV) solar system and EV wallbox. The goal was to build a highly responsive, localized monitoring system that aggregates industrial protocols (Modbus) and lightweight IoT messaging (MQTT) into a single centralized dashboard.



Multi-Protocol Data Ingestion: Real-time ingestion of solar inverter metrics via Modbus TCP/RTU and wallbox states via MQTT.
Secure Mesh Routing: All cross-device data traffic between the protocol gateway and the database is fully encrypted and routed over a secure Tailscale network.
Time-Series Storage: High-frequency data logging into InfluxDB, structured for rapid querying and long-term analysis.
UI Refresh Optimization: Fine-tuned panel-specific refresh rates to separate high-frequency live data from slow-moving aggregate metrics.





Data Sources: PV Inverter (Modbus) & EV Wallbox (MQTT)
Edge Collection: Raspberry Pi 2 (Acting as the field protocol gateway)
Central Database: Raspberry Pi 4 running InfluxDB v2 (Time-Series)
Network Overlay: Tailscale (Secure WireGuard-based overlay network)
Visualization: Grafana (Stat panels, time-series graphs, and custom dashboard layouts)




Solar Dashboard Demo

title: Solar PV Monitoring & Data Pipeline
text:
Data Pipeline Architecture
The system utilizes a split-architecture to handle field communications and data visualization efficiently and securely:

1. Protocol Ingestion (Raspberry Pi 2)
The Raspberry Pi 2 serves as the dedicated field gateway. It directly interfaces with the hardware components: * Modbus: Polled at regular intervals to extract active power output, voltage, and daily yield directly from the inverter. * MQTT: Subscribes to the wallbox topics to immediately catch state changes, current limits, and active charging sessions.


2. Secure Time-Series Storage (Raspberry Pi 4 via Tailscale)
Once the Pi 2 collects and normalizes the raw payloads, it transmits the data to InfluxDB v2 running on the Raspberry Pi 4. Instead of exposing ports locally or over a traditional VPN, the transmission relies entirely on Tailscale.
The Pi 2 securely pushes data straight to the Pi 4’s encrypted Tailscale IP over an isolated WireGuard tunnel. The data is organized into dedicated measurements (inverter and wallbox), enabling seamless time-series queries.


3. Frontend & Dashboard Optimization (Grafana)
Grafana sits on top of InfluxDB to render the visual panels. To prevent unnecessary browser rendering overhead and eliminate visual flickering, the UI is optimized using selective refresh intervals: * Live Panels: Instantaneous metrics like current consumption or PV generation update on a rapid cycle. * Aggregate Value Cards: Slower metrics like total daily yield are decoupled using now/d overrides and increased Min interval thresholds, completely hiding unnecessary loading indicators.
